title: Data Types and Variables
learningObjectives: Name all eight primitives, their default values, and when widening versus narrowing applies
Contrast primitives with wrappers, autoboxing costs, and Integer caching pitfalls
Choose BigDecimal versus double for money and long versus int for identifiers and counts
Predict narrowing truncation (for example int 300 cast to byte) and silent int overflow
Model banking-style fields with defensible types and document ranges in APIs
Demonstrate widening and narrowing conversions with deterministic printed output

sections.title: Why data types and variables matter in real systems
sections.content:
A ledger service ships a patch that stores daily transaction volume in an int because “we never get more than a million rows.” Six months later a marketing spike pushes ingest past two billion events in a reporting window. The counter wraps, dashboards show negative growth, and finance opens a severity-one because reconciliation no longer ties. The failure is not a mystery algorithm. It is a type range decision made without arithmetic on the worst day. The environment is a JVM service writing aggregates to a relational database. The lifecycle moment is sustained high throughput, not a single bad request.

Interviewers who probe primitives versus wrappers are not checking whether you memorized eight keywords. They want evidence that you understand representation: bits, default values, nullability, autoboxing cost, and the difference between value semantics for int and reference semantics for Integer. The gap between a weak answer and a strong one is whether you can name a concrete production symptom when those semantics collide, such as NullPointerException after unboxing a null Integer or wrong branch logic after comparing wrappers with == outside the cache range.

Use this four-step pattern whenever you justify a type. First, name the domain quantity and its legal range, including whether zero, negative, or null are meaningful. Second, name the Java type that matches that range with margin for growth, promoting to long or BigDecimal before the multiply, not after. Third, name a failure mode: overflow, truncation on narrowing cast, binary floating error, or null unboxing. Fourth, say how you verify: unit tests at boundaries, Math.addExact in hot paths, or schema documentation that states min, max, unit, and serialization format.

At scale, small type mistakes compound across services. One team exposes account identifiers as JSON numbers; JavaScript clients lose precision above 2^53 because Number is IEEE-754 double. Another team stores currency as double in DTOs; pennies drift in tax lines. A third team caches Integer instances and accidentally relies on identity equality for values outside -128 to 127, so two logical duplicates fail a deduplication check. Each bug traces back to the same root cause: the engineer treated types as interchangeable labels instead of contracts.

The answer that signals seniority ties types to observability and API design. You mention that public fields carrying money should be BigDecimal in the domain, serialized as string or integer cents at the edge, and never as raw double. You mention that counters and synthetic IDs that can exceed Integer.MAX_VALUE must be long everywhere they flow, including JDBC getters, message payloads, and UI models if the client can represent them. You mention that narrowing casts are acceptable only with explicit validation or proven invariants, because Java will not throw when high bits are discarded.

In daily work this topic shows up during code review, debugging, and on-call. A reviewer asks why a method returns Integer instead of int; you explain null as a tri-state signal versus Optional or a sentinel and the cost of accidental null unboxing. While debugging a flaky report, you discover byte casts from upstream integers that silently truncate. On-call you trace a payment mismatch to a single double addition in a discount path and propose migrating that module to BigDecimal with a controlled rollout. This day trains you to treat every variable declaration as a small design decision with measurable consequences.

sections.title: Theory and Internals — primitives, wrappers, widening, narrowing, and numeric contracts
sections.content:
### Plain-language overview

**Java** splits values into **primitive types** and **reference types**. Primitives encode numeric magnitude, truth, or a single UTF-16 code unit directly in the variable; references point at **objects** on the **heap**, including wrappers such as **Integer**, **String**, and domain classes. Choosing the right representation is not a style preference. It is how you express legal ranges, nullability, arithmetic rules, and serialization contracts to the compiler, to your teammates, and to every downstream service that reads your data.

### 1. The eight primitives and their defaults

Java defines **byte** (8-bit), **short** (16-bit), **int** (32-bit), **long** (64-bit), **float** (32-bit IEEE-754), **double** (64-bit IEEE-754), **char** (16-bit UTF-16 code unit), and **boolean** (true/false, size JVM-specific). Instance fields of these types receive **default values** (zero, false, or `\\u0000`) when uninitialized; **local variables** have no default and must be assigned before use, which the compiler enforces. **Interview angle:** Candidates who blur “default zero” for fields with “maybe zero” for locals fail simple screening questions—state the rule in one sentence and move on to a concrete bug it prevents.

| Type | Typical width | Default (field) | Common use |
|------|----------------|-----------------|------------|
| byte | 8-bit signed | 0 | binary buffers, tight arrays |
| short | 16-bit signed | 0 | legacy protocols, rare |
| int | 32-bit signed | 0 | default integral choice |
| long | 64-bit signed | 0L | large counts, time as epoch millis |
| float | 32-bit IEEE | 0.0f | graphics, ML features |
| double | 64-bit IEEE | 0.0d | scientific, stats (not money) |
| char | 16-bit UTF-16 | `\\u0000` | single code units, not full strings |
| boolean | JVM-defined | false | flags |

### 2. Wrapper classes and where they appear

Each primitive except **void** has a wrapper (**Byte**, **Short**, **Integer**, **Long**, **Float**, **Double**, **Character**, **Boolean**). Wrappers are **immutable objects**; **Integer** can be **null**, **int** cannot. **Generics** in Java erase to **Object** bounds, so `List<int>` is illegal and you write `List<Integer>`. **Interview angle:** Explain that nullability is the main API design fork—if absent values are impossible, prefer **int**; if absence matters, use **Integer** with discipline or model with **Optional** at boundaries.

### 3. Autoboxing and unboxing—convenience with a bill

The compiler inserts **valueOf**/**intValue**-style calls when you assign between primitives and wrappers. That convenience hides allocation and **NullPointerException** on unboxing. A hot loop that repeatedly boxes **int** into **Integer** can increase **GC** pressure enough to show up in production flame graphs. **Interview angle:** Say you would profile before micro-optimizing, but you know the escape hatch is primitive streams, specialized collections, or plain **int** locals.

```java
Integer a = 5;   // autobox
int b = a;       // unboxing; NPE if a == null
```

### 4. Widening conversions—silent and safe

**Widening** follows a defined order: **int** to **long** to **float** to **double**, and **int** to **double** directly, etc. The JVM converts without explicit cast when no precision loss occurs for the integral-to-integral widenings you are allowed. **Interview angle:** The subtle trap is **int** multiplication done **before** promotion—`long rows = count * width` can overflow in **int** land before the widen if both operands are **int**; cast or widen **first**.

### 5. Narrowing casts—truncation, not rounding

**Narrowing** requires an explicit cast. High-order bits drop away. The classic classroom example `int x = 300; byte b = (byte) x;` yields **44** because only the low eight bits survive. No exception throws. **Interview angle:** Tie this to binary protocols or careless downcasts from **long** database keys to **int** in DTOs—symptoms look like random small positives or negatives, not clean “overflow exceptions.”

### 6. Integer caching and **==** versus **equals**

**Integer.valueOf** caches a range (commonly **-128** to **127**). **==** compares references, so two **Integer** objects with value **200** may compare false while **100** compares true by accident. Always use **Objects.equals** or **intValue** comparison for values. **Interview angle:** This is a deliberate “gotcha” question—answer with cache range, then say you still never rely on **==** for wrappers in application logic.

### 7. Literals: suffixes and readability

Integer literals default to **int**; append **L** for **long**. Floating literals default to **double**; append **f** for **float**. Underscores in literals (Java 7+) improve readability: `1_000_000`. **Interview angle:** Mention compile-time errors when a literal exceeds **int** range without **L**—a real source of junior confusion in code review.

### 8. **double** versus **BigDecimal** for money

**double** stores binary fractions; many decimal values are not representable exactly, so `0.1 + 0.2` surprises beginners. **BigDecimal** with **String** constructors and explicit **scale** and **RoundingMode** models ledger math. **Interview angle:** Senior answers mention **rounding policy** (bankers vs half-up), **scale** for tax lines, and never serializing money as bare **double** across HTTP for public APIs.

```java
double d = 0.1 + 0.2;
// BigDecimal exact path:
// new BigDecimal("0.1").add(new BigDecimal("0.2"))
```

### 9. Integral overflow—wrap versus fail-fast

**int** arithmetic wraps modulo 2³². **Math.addExact**, **multiplyExact**, and **toIntExact** throw **ArithmeticException** on overflow—use them when silent wrap is unacceptable. **Interview angle:** Connect to metrics, counters, and ID generation; say you promote to **long** **before** multiplication, then optionally check with **exact** helpers.

### 10. **char**, **String**, and supplementary characters

**char** holds one UTF-16 code unit; some Unicode code points need a surrogate **pair**, so iteration by **char** alone can split emoji. Production code often uses **codePoint** APIs for correctness. **String** is immutable; variables hold references to shared character data. **Interview angle:** Do not claim **char** is “a character” in all human languages—phrase it as a UTF-16 code unit.

### 11. **var** for locals (Java 10+)

**var** asks the compiler to infer the type from the initializer. It keeps code concise but can hide accidental boxed types when the right-hand side returns **Integer**. Use it when the initializer makes the intent obvious. **Interview angle:** State that **var** cannot appear without an initializer and is not allowed for parameters.

### 12. Banking-style field choices (scenario table)

| Field | Suggested type | Rationale |
|-------|----------------|-----------|
| Account balance | **BigDecimal** | exact decimal money |
| Transactions per day | **int** or **long** | pick **long** if lifetime totals matter |
| Account holder name | **String** | textual identity |
| Active flag | **boolean** | two-state logic |
| Account ID > 2³¹ | **long** | exceeds **int** range |

**Interview angle:** Mention **null** meaning for **Boolean** in databases versus primitive **boolean**—tri-state flags need care.

### 13. API documentation for numeric fields

Public models should document **min**, **max**, **unit** (cents versus major currency), **overflow behavior**, and serialization shape. For cross-language clients, **IDs** that may exceed **2⁵³** should be **strings** in JSON. **Interview angle:** This is how you connect Java types to frontend **Number** limitations without hand-waving.

### 14. Sixty-second interview story

Java primitives give you fast, null-free arithmetic with fixed ranges; wrappers add **heap objects**, **null**, and **generics** interop. Widening is implicit and safe when you widen the operands early enough; narrowing is explicit and **silently truncates** high bits—validate before you cast. Money belongs in **BigDecimal** with string construction and explicit rounding, not in **double**. Counters that can exceed **Integer.MAX_VALUE** belong in **long** end to end. Compare wrapper **values** with **equals**, never **==**, because caching only covers a small range. When overflow must not happen silently, use **Math.addExact** or widen before you multiply. After stating that, you sound like someone who has debugged real ledgers, not just read a tutorial.

### 13b. Stack locals versus heap objects (mental model for interviews)

Local primitives live in **stack frames** and die when the frame pops; wrapper instances live on the **heap** until **GC** reclaims them. This distinction explains performance questions without pretending you always inspect assembly. A method that allocates a fresh **Integer** per loop iteration creates more **GC** traffic than one that accumulates in a primitive **int**. **Interview angle:** Pair this with **escape analysis** only if asked—many interviewers want the high-level story: primitives are dense values, wrappers are small immutable objects with headers.

### 15. Floating-point specials: Infinity and NaN

**double** division by zero yields **Infinity** or **NaN**, not a thrown **ArithmeticException** (integer division by zero still throws). **NaN** is not equal to itself with **==**; use **Double.isNaN**. Defensive financial code never relies on raw **double** comparisons without epsilon when the domain is continuous math, but money still prefers **BigDecimal**. **Interview angle:** Mention **IEEE-754** specials when debugging “why did my comparator sort break?”

### 16. Choosing **int** versus **long** for time and IDs

Epoch milliseconds and snowflake-style identifiers routinely exceed **int**. Store them as **long** in Java models, JDBC mappings, and message DTOs. **Interview angle:** If someone proposes **int** for “database auto-increment,” ask whether the table can exceed two billion rows or whether IDs are reassigned—migration pain arrives quietly.

| Situation | Prefer | Pitfall if wrong |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Daily page views under a billion | **int** | Promote if totals aggregate globally |
| Global impression counts | **long** | Silent **int** wrap in dashboards |
| Money with tax lines | **BigDecimal** | **double** drift in reconciliation |
| JSON to JS for huge IDs | **String** | Precision loss above 2⁵³ |

### Satyverse drill — tie-down

Reproduce three prints in one scratch class: widening **int** to **long**, narrowing **int** to **byte** with a value over **127**, and **BigDecimal** addition of **"0.1"** and **"0.2"**. Say aloud what you expect before you run. Then explain **why** the **byte** result is not an error at runtime. That loop converts facts into recall.

### 17. From assignment to discipline

Your Day 2 assignment chains four ideas: list primitives and contrast **int** with **Integer**, explain narrowing with **300 → (byte) → 44**, justify banking types from a scenario table, and print widening versus narrowing for **1_000_000**. Treat each as a **checklist** you can reuse in code review: ranges documented, casts justified, money exact, identifiers wide enough, and comparisons using **equals** for wrappers. **Interview angle:** Close answers by naming **one test** you would add—boundary literal, **addExact** guard, or serialization round-trip.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Using int for global counters or snowflake IDs — production symptom is negative totals or duplicate merges after wrap; detect via metrics anomalies and integration tests at Integer.MAX_VALUE boundaries; fix by promoting to long everywhere and backfilling schemas.

Storing currency in double — ledgers disagree by cents under load; detect with reconciliation jobs and property-based tests; fix by migrating domain math to BigDecimal with explicit scale and rounding mode.

Comparing Integer values with == outside the cache range — flaky equality in caches or dedup maps; detect with unit tests using 200 and 200; fix by using Objects.equals or primitive unbox after null check.
Narrowing long database keys to int in DTOs — silent truncation to garbage IDs; detect when clients log impossible small IDs; fix by mapping BIGINT to long end-to-end and rejecting overflow casts.
Multiplying two int quantities before promoting to long — overflow then widen is too late; detect with Math.addExact/multiplyExact in staging; fix by casting operands to long before the multiply.

Returning null Integer and unboxing to int — intermittent NullPointerException in business logic; detect with static analysis and Optional at boundaries; fix by using int with sentinel, or guard null explicitly.
Serializing large IDs as JSON numbers for JavaScript clients — user sees wrong account in UI; detect in browser console with rounded IDs; fix by encoding IDs as strings or using BigInt on the client.

Relying on double equality without epsilon for continuous math — rare branch flips in sorting or thresholds; detect with fuzzy tests; fix with Double.compare, tolerances, or decimal types suited to the domain.

sections.title: Exercise — Day 2 assignment: primitives, banking types, widening/narrowing
sections.problem:
Tie together the four Day 2 assignment themes in one `arch.day2` program named `Day2Exercise`.

Requirements:
1. Print a numbered list of all eight Java primitive types with their bit widths and one sentence on when you pick each (conceptual alignment with **Primitives vs Wrapper Classes**).
2. Demonstrate narrowing by casting `int 300` to `byte` and printing both values with a one-line explanation of the bit pattern (alignment with **Type Casting and Overflow**).
3. Print a small Markdown-style table (plain text is fine) choosing Java types for account balance, daily transaction count, holder name, active flag, and account ID that may exceed two billion—justify each in one line (alignment with **Choosing the Right Data Type**).
4. Start from `int x = 1_000_000;` — widen implicitly to `long` and `double`, then narrow explicitly to `short` and `byte`, printing all five values with labels (alignment with **Type Conversion Calculator**).
5. Add a comment noting that every step is O(1) time and O(1) extra space because only scalar conversions run.
hints: Use `Integer.valueOf` scenarios separately from `==` versus `equals` if you add a bonus print, but keep the required output deterministic.
For money in the table, insist on `BigDecimal` with a note about string constructors; never recommend `double` for balances.
When narrowing, call out that Java does not throw—your defence is range checks or wider types upstream.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: List Java primitive types and their main use cases.
conceptual.answer: For **List Java primitive types and their main use cases.**, start from JVM internals: primitive values are stored directly in local variable slots and on the operand stack, while references point to heap objects whose headers, alignment, and card-table writes affect GC behavior. That mechanical difference is why casual boxing or wide object graphs increase allocation rate and young-generation pressure in real services. In production, this shows up as higher p95 latency during burst traffic, elevated allocation rate in JFR, and occasional timeouts when stop-the-world pauses overlap with request spikes. The diagnostic path is concrete: run `jcmd <pid> GC.heap_info` for heap layout, `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 10` for GC churn, and `javap -c` on suspect classes to confirm implicit boxing or widening bytecode. Version behavior also matters: Java 8 defaults and ergonomics differ from Java 11 container-awareness, Java 17 UTF-8 default charset, and Java 21 runtime/JIT improvements. A Staff-level answer ties data type choice to observability, failure mode, and JVM-version rollout safety.

Prefer **concrete commands** (`javac`, `java`, `mvn test`) over vague theory.

seniorScenario.question: Counters in analytics hit negative values—suspected overflow. Fix?
seniorScenario.answer:
**Immediate response:** I acknowledge the incident, freeze risky deploys, and gather objective runtime evidence before debating code quality. I capture `jcmd <pid> VM.version`, `jcmd <pid> VM.flags`, `jcmd <pid> GC.heap_info`, and a short `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 20` sample, then verify whether the failure is active user impact, startup-only, or slow-burn degradation. I also compare canary versus stable pods so rollback decisions are data-driven in the first five minutes.

**Root cause:** For **Counters in analytics hit negative values—suspected overflow. Fix?**, the typical root cause is a mismatch between Java-level intent and JVM-level behavior: primitive vs boxed representations, class loading timing, bytecode verification, or classpath/module visibility. These issues are often latent until a specific traffic shape or deployment path executes the vulnerable branch. The JVM then surfaces it as throughput collapse, `NoClassDefFoundError`, `VerifyError`, or amplified GC pauses rather than a clean compile-time failure.

**Fix:** I implement a narrow, testable change and validate it with commands, not assumptions. Examples include replacing accidental boxing with primitive paths, correcting classpath/module flags, aligning JDK toolchains across CI and runtime, or adding explicit JVM flags for container memory behavior. I ship with a guarded rollout and verify post-change with `java -verbose:class` for loading correctness, `jcmd <pid> Compiler.queue` for warmup pressure, and GC/utilization deltas from `jstat`.

**Prevention:** I add guardrails in code review and pipelines: a checklist for data-type choices in hot paths, mandatory toolchain pinning, startup smoke tests that execute critical reflective/class-loading flows, and dashboards for allocation rate plus GC pause percentiles. I also record version-sensitive notes for Java 8, 11, 17, and 21 behavior so migrations are rehearsed with performance baselines, not treated as simple patch bumps.

These issues are often latent until a specific traffic shape or deployment path executes the vulnerable branch. The JVM then surfaces it as throughput collapse, `NoClassDefFoundError`, `VerifyError`, or amplified GC pauses rather than a clean compile-time failure.

**Fix:** I implement a narrow, testable change and validate it with commands, not assumptions. Examples include replacing accidental boxing with primitive paths, correcting classpath/module flags, aligning JDK toolchains across CI and runtime, or adding explicit JVM flags for container memory behavior. I ship with a guarded rollout and verify post-change with `java -verbose:class` for loading correctness, `jcmd <pid> Compiler.queue` for warmup pressure, and GC/utilization deltas from `jstat`.

**Prevention:** I add guardrails in code review and pipelines: a checklist for data-type choices in hot paths, mandatory toolchain pinning, startup smoke tests that execute critical reflective/class-loading flows, and dashboards for allocation rate plus GC pause percentiles. I also record version-sensitive notes for Java 8, 11, 17, and 21 behavior so migrations are rehearsed with performance baselines, not treated as simple patch bumps.

wrongAnswers: double is the best type for all currency calculations — **BigDecimal** with string constructors and explicit **RoundingMode** models ledger math; **double** is for continuous science, not balances.

Autoboxing means **Integer** and **int** are always interchangeable with no performance cost — hot loops that box repeatedly increase allocations; null **Integer** also explodes on unboxing with **NullPointerException**.
Casting **long** to **int** never loses data — narrowing drops high bits silently; huge positive **long** values become small or negative **int** values without throwing.
**Integer.parseInt("abc")** returns **0** — it throws **NumberFormatException**; always catch or validate input before parsing.
**char** is the same as **String** with one character — **char** is a primitive UTF-16 code unit; **String** is an object that can hold full text and supplementary characters.
**NaN == NaN** is true so **float** compares safely with **==** — **NaN** is never equal to itself with **==**; use **Float.isNaN** / **Double.isNaN**.
**byte** can store any **int** after a cast — **byte** only keeps eight bits; large **int** values truncate, which is why **300** becomes **44**.
**==** on **Integer** always compares numeric value — **==** compares references; use **equals** or primitive unbox after null checks.

sections.title: MCQ Drill - Data Types and Variables

questions.question: Which **primitive** type should you choose for a true/false feature flag value?

options.B: `char`

questions.explanation: `boolean` maps directly to boolean semantics and avoids parsing overhead. A is correct. B is wrong because `char` stores code units, not logical truth values; C is wrong because `byte` needs custom conventions and hurts readability; D is wrong because `String` adds heap allocations and comparison pitfalls. In production this matters because misusing strings for flags causes avoidable parsing bugs and GC noise in hot request paths.

questions.question: What is the size of Java **`int`** and what numeric form does it use?

options.B: 32-bit two's complement

questions.explanation: `int` is fixed as 32-bit two's-complement by the Java Language Specification. B is correct. A is wrong because 16-bit corresponds to `short`, not `int`; C is wrong because 64-bit floating point is `double`; D is wrong because Java primitive widths are specification-defined, not OS-dependent. In production this matters because wrong assumptions break serialization contracts and database range checks.

options.B: `6` then `6`

questions.explanation: Post-increment returns the old value first, then increments. A is correct. B is wrong because that would be pre-increment behavior (`++x`); C is wrong because increment does happen after the first print; D is wrong because the code is syntactically and semantically valid. In production this matters because operator misuse in counters can create off-by-one bugs in rate limiting and pagination.

`+=` performs an implicit cast back to `byte`, causing overflow wrap-around. B is correct. A is wrong because a `byte` cannot represent 140 directly; C is wrong because compound assignment includes narrowing conversion automatically; D is wrong because 130 still exceeds byte range and cannot be stored as-is. In production this matters because silent overflow can corrupt totals and metering counters in billing systems.

questions.question: Why is **autoboxing** from `int` to `Integer` risky in hot loops?

options.B: It adds heap allocations and potential GC pressure

questions.explanation: Boxing can allocate objects and stress young generation under throughput load. B is correct. A is wrong because JIT still runs; it just optimizes around heavier allocation patterns; C is wrong because numeric semantics remain integer-based; D is wrong because boxing has no relation to checked-exception rules. In production this matters because unnecessary boxing raises allocation rate and degrades latency under peak traffic.

questions.question: A junior engineer stores monetary values in `float` and reports rounding drift in invoices. What is the best correction?

options.B: Use `int` cents or `BigDecimal` with explicit scale
options.D: Keep `float` and round at display only

questions.explanation: Money requires deterministic precision, so integer cents or `BigDecimal` is the safe model. B is correct. A is wrong because `double` is still binary floating point and can drift; C is wrong because character storage adds complexity without numerical guarantees; D is wrong because display rounding does not fix persisted arithmetic errors. In production this matters because precision drift causes financial reconciliation defects and audit failures.

`char` stores UTF-16 code units, and 65 maps to `A`. B is correct. A is wrong because that would print if `c` were an integer type; C is wrong because numeric literal to `char` in range is valid; D is wrong because 97 maps to lowercase `a`, not 65. In production this matters because wrong char/code-point assumptions break parsing and protocol framing.

questions.question: Which command best confirms the JVM version of a **running** process?

options.B: `jcmd <pid> VM.version`

questions.explanation: `jcmd <pid> VM.version` inspects the live process directly. B is correct. A is wrong because local shell version may differ from deployed runtime; C is wrong because compiler version does not prove runtime VM version; D is wrong because thread dump alone does not report full runtime version metadata. In production this matters because assuming wrong runtime version leads to faulty migration and tuning decisions.

questions.question: What is the key runtime difference between `int[]` and `Integer[]`?

options.B: `Integer[]` stores object references and adds heap/object-header overhead

questions.explanation: `int[]` is contiguous primitive storage, while `Integer[]` points to boxed objects. B is correct. A is wrong because layout and allocation behavior remain materially different; C is wrong because both arrays are iterable; D is wrong because `Integer[]` can contain nulls by design. In production this matters because wrong array type choices inflate memory and GC pause times in data-heavy workloads.

options.B: `false` always

questions.explanation: `Integer.valueOf` caches -128..127 by default, so references match here. A is correct. B is wrong because for cached values, references can be equal; C is wrong because code is valid Java; D is wrong because comparison prints boolean, not null. In production this matters because reference-equality misuse causes inconsistent bugs when values cross cache boundaries.

The multiplication occurs in `int` first, overflows, then widens to `long`. B is correct. A is wrong because you need at least one long literal (`1_000_000_000L`) for that result; C is wrong because overflow is runtime arithmetic behavior, not compile-time syntax failure; D is wrong because overflow does not collapse to zero here. In production this matters because overflow-before-widening corrupts counters and SLA reporting calculations.

questions.question: When migrating from Java 8 to Java 17, which data-handling change often surprises teams?

options.B: Default charset becomes UTF-8 in Java 17

questions.explanation: JDK 17 standardizes default charset as UTF-8, affecting implicit encoding behavior. B is correct. A is wrong because primitive widths remain the same; C is wrong because boolean model is unchanged; D is wrong because `char` remains 16-bit UTF-16 code unit. In production this matters because implicit charset assumptions can break file parsing and message signatures after upgrade.

questions.question: A service shows rising GC pauses after a new feature that maps millions of IDs. Which first remediation is most appropriate?

options.A: Convert hot-path `Integer` collections to primitive-friendly structures
options.B: Increase thread pool size first

questions.explanation: Reducing boxing/object count addresses root allocation pressure directly. A is correct. B is wrong because more threads can worsen allocation/GC contention; C is wrong because hiding telemetry does not solve memory churn; D is wrong because floating point substitution is unrelated and risky. In production this matters because ignoring allocation root cause leads to recurring pause spikes and unstable latency.

options.B: `false`

questions.explanation: Binary floating-point cannot represent many decimals exactly. B is correct. A is wrong because exact decimal equality is not guaranteed for doubles; C is wrong because the expression is valid; D is wrong because the program prints a boolean from equality comparison. In production this matters because naive floating-point equality checks can misroute financial or threshold logic.

options.B: Fails because `s + 1` is promoted to `int`

questions.explanation: Binary numeric promotion makes the result `int`, requiring explicit cast or `+=`. B is correct. A is wrong because assignment without cast is invalid; C is wrong because promotion rules are not Java-8-only; D is wrong because `var` does not alter arithmetic promotion semantics. In production this matters because type-promotion misunderstandings cause compile churn and risky casts in critical code.

questions.question: Why should architecture reviews discuss primitive choice in DTOs and caches?

options.B: Type representation changes memory footprint, serialization, and GC profile
options.C: Because JVM ignores type at runtime

questions.explanation: Type decisions directly impact runtime layout and operational behavior. B is correct. A is wrong because it is primarily a performance and correctness concern; C is wrong because the JVM absolutely uses type metadata and layout details; D is wrong because type decisions increase, not remove, need for tests. In production this matters because poor type decisions cascade into latency regressions and infra cost overruns.

options.B: `a.equals(b)`

questions.explanation: `equals` compares values, while `==` compares references. B is correct. A is wrong because reference identity fails outside cache ranges and across allocations; C is wrong because comparing to null is invalid and can throw; D is wrong because hash collisions and indirection make this incorrect for equality logic. In production this matters because incorrect equality checks create inconsistent behavior in deduplication and authorization maps.

questions.question: `UnsupportedClassVersionError` appears in staging after merge. What is the most likely root cause?

options.A: Runtime JDK is older than class file target version
options.B: GC algorithm changed

questions.explanation: Class major version exceeds what runtime VM supports. A is correct. B is wrong because GC changes do not produce class-version loader errors; C is wrong because DB connectivity does not affect bytecode major version checks; D is wrong because thread pools do not influence class loader compatibility. In production this matters because version mismatch blocks startup and can trigger failed rollout windows.

questions.question: Your build compiles locally but fails in CI saying `javac` not found. What should be standardized first?

options.B: Pin JDK toolchain and CI base image to JDK (not JRE)

questions.explanation: Toolchain and image pinning remove environment drift at the source. B is correct. A is wrong because retries cannot fix missing compiler binaries; C is wrong because warnings are unrelated to absent toolchain; D is wrong because tests cannot run if code cannot compile. In production this matters because unmanaged toolchains waste pipeline time and delay incident-critical fixes.

questions.question: Which command is best to inspect class loading during startup debugging?

options.B: `javac -Xlint`

questions.explanation: `-verbose:class` shows each class and load source, ideal for classpath issues. A is correct. B is wrong because compile-time lint does not expose runtime loading sources; C is wrong because heap histograms do not provide class-load order/source details; D is wrong because thread dumps omit classpath/load-path resolution. In production this matters because without load-source visibility, classpath conflicts linger and cause fragile deployments.

questions.question: On-call: p99 latency jumped from 120ms to 900ms after switching a hot cache key from `long` to `Long`. Best first action?

options.A: Rollback and capture allocation/GC evidence with `jstat` and `jcmd`
options.B: Increase request timeout to 5s

questions.explanation: Boxing can amplify allocation rate, so rollback plus GC diagnostics is fastest risk-reducing path. A is correct. B is wrong because timeouts hide impact but do not resolve memory churn; C is wrong because database scaling is orthogonal to in-process allocation inflation; D is wrong because removing logs harms incident diagnosis. In production this matters because failing to address boxing-induced churn prolongs user-visible latency incidents.

questions.question: Architecture review: team wants `double` for currency to simplify math. What is staff-level decision?

options.B: Reject; use `BigDecimal` or scaled integers with explicit rounding policy
options.D: Store as `String` and parse per request

questions.explanation: Financial correctness requires deterministic decimal behavior and explicit rounding control. B is correct. A is wrong because binary floating-point drift is unacceptable for money; C is wrong because `float` is less precise and makes errors worse; D is wrong because string parsing adds latency and still needs numeric policy. In production this matters because poor monetary representation leads to billing defects, disputes, and compliance risk.

questions.question: Migration to Java 21: one service starts failing with encoding mismatches in downstream signatures. Most likely preventive control?

options.A: Rely on platform default charset everywhere
options.B: Standardize explicit UTF-8 in APIs, files, and tests

questions.explanation: Explicit charset declarations remove environment/version ambiguity during migration. B is correct. A is wrong because implicit defaults vary across versions and environments; C is wrong because downgrade avoids the symptom but not engineering discipline; D is wrong because heap size does not fix encoding semantics. In production this matters because charset drift breaks signatures and cross-system data integrity.

questions.question: A canary fails only under traffic with `NoClassDefFoundError` for an optional module class. What should be fixed first?

options.B: Align runtime classpath/module packaging with exercised code path

questions.explanation: Lazy class loading exposes packaging mistakes when that branch is first executed. B is correct. A is wrong because resource limits do not create missing bytecode; C is wrong because suppressing checks allows broken artifacts to reach production; D is wrong because language rewrite is not immediate incident remediation. In production this matters because classpath drift causes partial outages that evade unit tests.

questions.question: Post-release memory costs rose 30% after switching DTO IDs from `int` to `Integer` for nullability. Best architectural adjustment?

options.B: Model nullability at boundaries; keep hot internal paths primitive

questions.explanation: Boundary nullability can be preserved while avoiding boxed overhead in core processing. B is correct. A is wrong because cost is often reducible through representation design; C is wrong because `Object` increases casting and runtime risk; D is wrong because removing telemetry does not control memory footprint. In production this matters because unbounded object overhead increases infra spend and GC pause variance.

questions.question: Incident command asks for fastest proof that runtime JDK differs from build JDK. Which evidence is strongest?

options.B: `jcmd <pid> VM.version` from live process plus CI build logs

questions.explanation: Live process metadata combined with build logs proves end-to-end version mismatch. B is correct. A is wrong because documentation can be stale; C is wrong because local environment is not authoritative for deployed runtime; D is wrong because comments are not runtime evidence. In production this matters because without hard evidence, teams misdiagnose startup failures and prolong MTTR.

questions.question: A high-throughput service uses `Map<String, Object>` for numeric values and performs casts per request. Staff-level refactor?

options.B: Introduce typed domain model with primitive fields in hot paths
options.C: Wrap casts in try/catch and ignore failures

questions.explanation: Typed models reduce casting, improve JIT optimization, and prevent runtime type faults. B is correct. A is wrong because dynamic maps trade short-term flexibility for long-term runtime cost; C is wrong because exception-driven flow is slow and masks data quality issues; D is wrong because backend still needs robust typed validation. In production this matters because untamed dynamic typing causes latency spikes and production ClassCastException paths.

questions.question: During Java 17 migration, reflective frameworks fail with `InaccessibleObjectException`. Correct mitigation path?

options.A: Disable module system checks globally forever
options.B: Add scoped `--add-opens` where needed and track removal plan

questions.explanation: Scoped opens restore compatibility while preserving controlled encapsulation strategy. B is correct. A is wrong because blanket disabling undermines security and maintainability; C is wrong because weakening encapsulation in code is unnecessary and risky; D is wrong because source level does not resolve runtime reflective access policy. In production this matters because improper mitigation creates security debt and brittle upgrade paths.

questions.question: A payment service intermittently overflows counters after traffic surge. Which redesign is most robust?

options.B: Use `long`/`BigInteger` where required with overflow tests and alerts
options.C: Store counters in `float` for larger range

questions.explanation: Capacity-safe numeric types plus detection controls prevent silent corruption. B is correct. A is wrong because overflow risk remains unresolved; C is wrong because floating point introduces precision and semantics problems; D is wrong because silent resets hide data loss and break auditability. In production this matters because counter overflow can underbill, mis-throttle, or break fraud-detection logic.

questions.question: A platform team wants one universal JVM image for Java 8, 11, 17, and 21 services. What is the architect response?

options.A: Approve; one image always reduces risk
options.B: Use per-major-version pinned runtime images with shared hardening baseline
options.C: Run Java 21 for all binaries regardless of bytecode target

questions.explanation: Pinning by major version preserves compatibility while retaining standardized security controls. B is correct. A is wrong because unified images can break compatibility and observability assumptions; C is wrong because runtime upgrades are not universally safe without validation; D is wrong because removing checks increases drift and outage probability. In production this matters because version ambiguity creates startup failures and migration regressions across fleets.

sections.title: Data types — interview cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| Eight primitives | Default zero/false; locals must be assigned | "Primitives are dense values with fixed ranges and no null." |
| int default | First choice for integral math that fits 32 bits | "I pick **int** until the domain proves it needs **long**." |
| long | >2³¹ counts, epoch millis, wide IDs | "Anything that can cross **Integer.MAX_VALUE** stays **long** end-to-end." |
| double vs BigDecimal | double is binary float; money needs decimal | "Ledgers use **BigDecimal**; **double** is for measurements and stats." |
| Widening | Implicit, promote before multiply | "I widen operands before the operation so **int** overflow cannot sneak in." |
| Narrowing cast | Truncates high bits, no exception | "Narrowing is explicit; I validate ranges or keep wider types." |
| Wrapper null | Integer can be null; int cannot | "Nullability drives API design between **int** and **Integer**." |
| == vs equals | Never use == for wrapper values | "I compare boxed values with **equals** or primitives after null checks." |
| Integer cache | Only -128..127 for valueOf identity games | "I never rely on **==** for **Integer**; caching is an optimization detail." |
| Literals | L for long, f for float | "Oversized literals need **L** or they fail at compile time." |
| var | Infers from initializer | "**var** is fine when the rhs makes the type obvious." |
| char vs code point | char is one UTF-16 unit | "Supplementary characters may need **codePoint** APIs, not raw **char** iteration." |
| Parse input | parseInt throws on bad text | "I validate strings before parsing and handle **NumberFormatException**." |
| JSON to JS | Number loses >2⁵³ integers | "Huge IDs become **String** on the wire to protect browsers." |
| Overflow | int wraps silently | "I use **long** promotion or **Math.addExact** when wrap is unacceptable." |
